key_concepts.trading: The buying and selling of various financial assets to make a profit based on fluctuations in the prices of those assets.
key_concepts.financial_asset: Any asset that has a financial value and can be traded. Includes currency pairs, derivatives, stocks, bonds, commodities.
key_concepts.long_position: Speculating that an asset's value will increase — enter at a low price (buy), exit at a higher price.
key_concepts.short_position: Speculating that an asset's value will decline — enter at the highest possible price (sell), exit at the lowest possible price.
key_concepts.leverage: Borrowed capital that allows you to open large positions with little equity. Multiplies both potential profits AND potential losses.
key_concepts.investor: Purchases a financial asset at a favourable price expecting long-term appreciation. Becomes the OWNER of the asset. May earn dividends or coupon payments.
key_concepts.trader: Seeks profit through speculation, usually in the short to medium term. Does NOT own the underlying asset. Can trade long or short freely.
key_concepts.volatility: A measure of the fluctuations in the price of financial assets. Higher volatility instruments (currency pairs, derivatives) suit short-term trading.
key_concepts.stop_loss: The maximum acceptable loss per trade — set before entry to limit downside.
key_concepts.take_profit: The desired profit level — the price at which a trade is closed with a gain.
key_concepts.demo_account: A simulated trading environment using virtual money. Used to practice without financial risk before live trading.
key_concepts.dividend: Regular return paid to shareholders by a company — only relevant to investors who own the stock.
key_concepts.coupon: Regular annual interest payment received by bondholders.

key_rules: Trading is simple but not easy — requires consistent discipline, preparation, and skill development.
The 90/90/90 rule: 90% of traders lose 90% of their invested capital within 90 days.
Excessively high earnings expectations are a recipe for long-term failure.
Most traders lose money in the beginning — this is a hard statistical fact, not an exception.
FTMO recommends starting on a demo account (FTMO Free Trial) before trading with real money.
With 100:1 leverage, $1,000 controls a $100,000 EURUSD position (1 lot).
Without leverage, trading 1 lot of EURUSD requires $100,000 in capital.
Anyone can become a trader regardless of education, nationality, wealth, or gender.
Success in trading is determined solely by attitude and ability — markets are non-discriminatory.
A trader can endure 5–10 consecutive losing trades and must be mentally prepared for this.
Discipline is a prerequisite for success — without it you cannot climb out of mediocrity.
Humility is essential — a humble trader admits mistakes and learns from them.
Trading is a never-ending cycle: skill development → mental preparation → application → analysis → improvement.
FTMO explicitly states: claims of ~100% success rate by trading gurus are 'unrealistic hogwash'.

trader.goal: Short to medium-term profit through speculation

five_essential_trader_qualities.description: Endure series of 5–10 consecutive losses without abandoning the strategy. Good and bad periods happen even to consistent traders.
Avoid constantly changing approach and strategy. Many successful traders are athletes trained in discipline from a young age.
Admit mistakes, learn from them, do not overestimate abilities. A humble trader keeps emotions in check and does not blame others for losses.
One of the MOST IMPORTANT factors for forex success. Self-knowledge and self-development are key. FTMO dedicates an extensive chapter to this.
Without enthusiasm for the subject, persistence alone fades. Constant learning and skill improvement are required.

leverage_example.formula: Required margin = Position size / Leverage ratio

exam_qa.q: What is trading?
exam_qa.a: The buying and selling of financial assets to make a profit based on price fluctuations.
exam_qa.why: Exact FTMO Academy definition from 'What is trading?' section.

exam_qa.q: What is the key difference between a trader and an investor?
exam_qa.a: A trader does not own the underlying asset and speculates in the short to medium term; an investor owns the asset and seeks long-term appreciation.
exam_qa.why: From 'Investing vs Trading' section — the distinction is ownership of the asset and time horizon.

exam_qa.q: In a long position, how does the trader make a profit?
exam_qa.a: By entering (buying) at a low price and exiting at a higher price.
exam_qa.why: 'In a long position, traders speculate on the asset's value increasing. They aim to enter the position at a low price (buy) and exit at a higher price.'

exam_qa.q: In a short position, how does the trader make a profit?
exam_qa.a: By entering (selling) at the highest possible price and exiting at the lowest possible price.
exam_qa.why: 'When speculating on a decline in the value of an asset (short position), they enter the position at the highest possible price (sell) and exit the position at the lowest possible price.'

exam_qa.q: What is leverage?
exam_qa.a: Borrowed capital that allows traders to open large positions with very little equity, multiplying both potential profits and potential losses.
exam_qa.why: From 'Investing vs Trading' section: 'Leverage allows you to open large positions with very little equity thanks to borrowed capital.'

exam_qa.q: How much capital is needed to trade 1 lot of EURUSD with 100:1 leverage?
exam_qa.why: FTMO Academy example: without leverage = $100,000; with 100:1 leverage = $1,000.

exam_qa.q: What is the 90/90/90 rule?
exam_qa.a: 90% of traders will lose 90% of their invested capital within 90 days.
exam_qa.why: 'The 90/90/90 rule states that 90% of traders will lose 90% of their invested capital within 90 days.'

exam_qa.q: According to FTMO, who can become a trader?
exam_qa.a: Anyone — regardless of education, nationality, location, family wealth, or gender. Outcomes are determined solely by attitude and ability.
exam_qa.why: 'According to FTMO data, traders come from diverse backgrounds... outcomes are determined solely by one's attitude and ability.'

exam_qa.q: What does FTMO say about high earnings expectations?
exam_qa.a: Excessively high expectations are a recipe for long-term failure. Traders must be realistic.
exam_qa.why: 'Having excessively high expectations is a recipe for long-term failure.'

exam_qa.q: What is a Stop Loss?
exam_qa.a: The maximum acceptable loss per trade — set before entry to limit downside.
exam_qa.why: Defined in 'Where to Start?' section when describing demo account practice.

exam_qa.q: What is a Take Profit?
exam_qa.a: The desired profit level — the price target at which a trade is automatically closed with a gain.
exam_qa.why: Defined in 'Where to Start?' section alongside Stop Loss.

exam_qa.q: Why are currency pairs and derivatives suitable for short-term trading?
exam_qa.a: Because of their higher volatility — they experience larger price fluctuations that traders can capitalize on in short timeframes.
exam_qa.why: 'Due to their higher volatility (a measure of the fluctuations in the price of financial assets), currency pairs and derivatives are suitable for these short-term trades.'

exam_qa.q: Which of the following is NOT listed by FTMO as an essential trader quality?
exam_qa.a: Mathematical excellence — FTMO explicitly says you do NOT need to excel at mathematics, macroeconomics, or chess.
exam_qa.why: 'Most people think that only someone who knows macroeconomics and microeconomics, excels in mathematics and is great at chess can become a trader. The reality is that you need slightly different qualities.'

exam_qa.q: Why does discipline help traders according to FTMO?
exam_qa.a: Discipline prevents constantly changing approach and strategy when things feel like they're not working, avoiding mediocrity.
exam_qa.why: 'Discipline will help you avoid the phase where you constantly change your approach and strategy because you feel that it's not working.'

exam_qa.q: What is volatility?
exam_qa.a: A measure of the fluctuations in the price of financial assets.
exam_qa.why: Exact FTMO Academy definition in the 'Investing vs Trading' section.

exam_qa.q: What does FTMO recommend before transitioning to real trading?
exam_qa.a: Practice on a demo account (FTMO Free Trial) to familiarise yourself with the platform, execute trades under real market conditions, and test basic strategies.
exam_qa.why: 'Before transitioning to real trading after studying our materials, practice with mock trading on a demo account.'

exam_qa.q: Trading is a never-ending process of:
exam_qa.a: Learning and improving skills, with consistency as the ultimate goal — in attitude, preparation, daily routine, and profitable trades.
exam_qa.why: 'It will be a never-ending process of learning and improving your skills with one goal, and that is consistency.'

exam_qa.q: What additional return can investors earn beyond price appreciation?
exam_qa.a: Dividends (from stocks) and coupon payments (from bonds).
exam_qa.why: 'Additionally, investors may earn regular returns from owning the asset itself... dividends... bondholders receive regular annual interest payments in the form of coupons.'

Psychological well-being is FTMO's most important factor — the agent's daily halt after losses is a psychological protection mechanism endorsed by FTMO.
Demo account (Free Trial) first — validates the current strategy of trading on FTMO Free Trial before paying for the real challenge.
Unrealistic expectations cause failure — the agent should reinforce patient, process-focused trading over profit-chasing behavior.

key_concepts.forex: Short for FOReign EXchange. A decentralised global market where all the world's currencies are traded. Operates as an over-the-counter (OTC) market — no centralised location. Largest and most liquid market globally with a daily trading volume exceeding $7.5 trillion (as of 2022).
key_concepts.currency_pair: Two different currencies quoted against each other. The first currency is the BASE currency; the second is the QUOTED currency (benchmark). The exchange rate indicates how many units of the quoted currency are needed to purchase ONE unit of the base currency.
key_concepts.base_currency: The first currency in a currency pair (e.g. EUR in EUR/USD). The exchange rate shows how many units of the quoted currency are needed to buy one unit of this currency.
key_concepts.quoted_currency: The second currency in a currency pair (e.g. USD in EUR/USD). Acts as the REFERRING benchmark. NOTE: FTMO uses 'referring benchmark' — not 'basic benchmark'. Exam trap.
key_concepts.leverage: Using borrowed capital to amplify the size of a trading account and positions. Allows traders to execute trades with significantly more capital than they actually have. Increases both potential profits AND potential losses.
key_concepts.margin: A deposit or advance held by a broker where a trader has an account. Enables traders to open positions on currency pairs or other instruments. Serves as assurance that the trader has enough funds to cover potential losses.
key_concepts.lot: A standardised measure of the quantity of a financial instrument available for trading. In forex, the standard lot size is 100,000 units of a particular currency.
key_concepts.pip: Price Interest Point (or Points in Percentage). The fundamental unit of currency movement in Forex. Represents 1/10,000th (0.0001) of the currency rate. Example: EUR/USD moving from 1.1000 to 1.1001 = 1 pip.
key_concepts.bid: The price at which a trader is willing to BUY a particular asset.
key_concepts.ask: The price at which a trader is willing to SELL an asset.
key_concepts.spread: The difference between the buy (bid) and sell (ask) prices.
key_concepts.long: Buying an asset with the expectation that its price will rise. The trader buys intending to sell later at a higher price.
key_concepts.short: Selling an asset with the expectation that its price will fall. The trader sells now intending to buy back later at a lower price.
key_concepts.buy_order: An order to buy a financial asset to speculate on the appreciation/increase of the exchange rate or price.
key_concepts.sell_order: An order to sell a financial asset to speculate on the depreciation/decline of the exchange rate or price.
key_concepts.market_order: An order that executes the trade at the best available price at the time of placing the order.
key_concepts.limit_order: An order to execute a trade at a more FAVOURABLE price than the current market price. For a LONG position: placed BELOW current price (wait for price to drop). For a SHORT position: placed ABOVE current price. May not execute if the price only briefly touches the level.
key_concepts.stop_order: Used when anticipating the CONTINUATION of current price movements. For a LONG: placed ABOVE current price. For a SHORT: placed BELOW current price. Acts as a market order when triggered — subject to slippage in low-liquidity or volatile markets.
key_concepts.stop_limit_order: A combination of Stop and Limit orders. Improved version of a Stop order that avoids slippage by triggering a Limit price when the Stop level is reached. The Limit price is the 'worst' price the trader is willing to accept. May not execute if price doesn't reach the Limit level after triggering the Stop.
key_concepts.stop_loss: A type of Stop order that closes a position when price moves AGAINST the trader. Represents the maximum loss the trader is willing to accept on a trade. For a long: placed BELOW entry price. Can be adjusted (trailed) to lock in profits as the trade moves in favour.
key_concepts.take_profit: A Limit order that closes an open position when price reaches the trader's target profit level. For a long: placed ABOVE entry price. For a short: placed BELOW entry price. Can be adjusted as the trade progresses.
key_concepts.trailing_stop: A dynamic Stop Loss that adjusts automatically according to the asset's price movement in the trader's favour. Activates only when a predetermined profit level is reached. Example: Trailing Stop at 25 points — once 25 points of profit is achieved, the SL moves to Break Even; it continues adjusting with each further favourable move. If no Take Profit is set, the trade stays open until price reverses 25 points.
key_concepts.candlestick_chart: A visual depiction of price fluctuations. Shows the trading range within a specified timeframe: open, close, low, and high prices. The candle colour indicates whether price increased (typically green/white) or decreased (typically red/black) during that period.
key_concepts.volatility: The degree of price fluctuation of a given asset. Reflects the degree of uncertainty and risk associated with an investment. Greater volatility = greater price range. High volatility means wider swings; low volatility means tighter, calmer price movement.
key_concepts.broker: A company that arranges trades in the financial markets for traders in return for a commission.
key_concepts.modern_prop_trading: Prop trading firms that seek out novice traders and provide them with tools, education, and environment to develop trading skills. They identify and educate potential traders without financial risk to their clients.

key_rules: Forex is OTC (over-the-counter) — no centralised exchange location.
Forex daily volume: $7.5 trillion (as of 2022) — largest and most liquid market globally.
Standard forex lot = 100,000 units of the base currency.
1 pip = 0.0001 = 1/10,000th of the currency rate.
Bid = buy price; Ask = sell price; Spread = Ask minus Bid.
Limit Order is placed at a MORE FAVOURABLE price than current; Stop Order anticipates CONTINUATION of current move.
Stop Order acts as a market order when triggered — beware slippage in volatile conditions.
Stop Limit Order adds a Limit level to a Stop to control maximum slippage — 'worst price willing to accept'.
Trailing Stop is DYNAMIC — moves automatically with price in the trader's favour; activates only after predetermined profit threshold.
Stop Loss for long position = placed BELOW entry price.
Take Profit for long position = placed ABOVE entry price.
Stop Loss for short position = placed ABOVE entry price.
Take Profit for short position = placed BELOW entry price.
EUR/USD long position = speculating EUR appreciates and/or USD depreciates.
EUR/USD short position = speculating EUR depreciates and/or USD appreciates.
Candlestick shows four price points: open, close, high, low — within a specified timeframe.
Broker earns commission — not the same as a prop firm.

exam_qa.q: What does Forex stand for?
exam_qa.a: FOReign EXchange.
exam_qa.why: 'Short for FOReign EXchange, it is a decentralised global market where all the world's currencies are traded.'

exam_qa.q: What type of market is Forex?
exam_qa.a: An over-the-counter (OTC) decentralised market — there is no centralised location.
exam_qa.why: 'It operates as an over-the-counter market, meaning there is no centralised location.'

exam_qa.q: What is the daily trading volume of the Forex market (as of 2022)?
exam_qa.a: Exceeding $7.5 trillion.
exam_qa.why: 'As the largest and most liquid market globally, it boasts a daily trading volume exceeding $7.5 trillion (as of 2022).'

exam_qa.q: In the EUR/USD pair, which is the base currency and which is the quoted currency?
exam_qa.a: EUR is the base currency; USD is the quoted currency.
exam_qa.why: 'The first currency of the pair is known as the base currency... in the EUR/USD pair, the euro (EUR) is the base currency, and the US dollar (USD) is the quoted currency.'

exam_qa.q: What is the purpose of a quoted currency in a currency pair?
exam_qa.a: The quoted currency is the second currency in a currency pair and serves as a REFERRING benchmark.
exam_qa.why: Confirmed correct on FTMO exam 2026-05-09. FTMO's wording is 'referring benchmark' — NOT 'basic benchmark'. The quoted currency is always the SECOND currency (not the first).
exam_qa.exam_trap: Both 'referring' and 'basic' sound plausible — FTMO chose 'referring'. Selecting 'basic benchmark' is WRONG even though the lesson text just says 'acting as the benchmark' without a qualifier.

exam_qa.q: What does the exchange rate of a currency pair indicate?
exam_qa.a: How many units of the quoted currency are required to purchase one unit of the base currency.
exam_qa.why: 'The exchange rate of a currency pair indicates how many units of the quoted currency are required to purchase one unit of the base currency.'

exam_qa.q: What is Margin?
exam_qa.a: A deposit or advance held by a broker that enables traders to open positions and serves as assurance they have enough funds to cover potential losses.
exam_qa.why: 'Margin, in simple terms, is a deposit or advance held by a broker... serves as assurance that they have enough funds to cover potential losses.'

exam_qa.q: What is the standard lot size in forex trading?
exam_qa.a: 100,000 units of a particular currency.
exam_qa.why: 'In forex trading, the standard lot size is 100,000 units of a particular currency.'

exam_qa.q: What is a pip?
exam_qa.a: Price Interest Point (or Points in Percentage) — the fundamental unit of currency movement in Forex, representing 1/10,000th of the currency rate.
exam_qa.why: 'Pip (Price Interest Point, or Points in Percentage) is the fundamental unit of currency movement in Forex, representing 1/10,000th of the currency rate.'

exam_qa.q: If EUR/USD moves from 1.1000 to 1.1001, how many pips did it move?
exam_qa.a: 1 pip.
exam_qa.why: 'If the EUR/USD price shifts from 1.1000 to 1.1001, this reflects a movement of 1 pip.'

exam_qa.q: What is the Bid price?
exam_qa.a: The price at which a trader is willing to BUY a particular asset.
exam_qa.why: Exact FTMO Academy definition: 'The price at which a trader is willing to buy a particular asset.'

exam_qa.q: What is the Ask price?
exam_qa.a: The price at which a trader is willing to SELL an asset.
exam_qa.why: Exact FTMO Academy definition: 'The price at which a trader is willing to sell an asset.'

exam_qa.q: What is the Spread?
exam_qa.a: The difference between the buy (bid) and sell (ask) prices.
exam_qa.why: Exact FTMO Academy definition: 'The difference between the buy (bid) and sell (ask) prices.'

exam_qa.q: What is a long position?
exam_qa.a: Buying an asset expecting its price to rise — buy intending to sell later at a higher price.
exam_qa.why: 'Long position means buying an asset with the expectation that its price will rise... the trader buys with the intention of selling later at a higher price.'

exam_qa.q: What is a short position?
exam_qa.a: Selling an asset expecting its price to fall — sell now intending to buy back later at a lower price.
exam_qa.why: 'Short position means selling an asset with the expectation that its price will fall... selling now with the intention of buying back later at a lower price.'

exam_qa.q: What is a Market Order?
exam_qa.a: An order that executes the trade at the best available price at the time of placing the order.
exam_qa.why: 'A Market order means the trader will execute the trade at the best available price at the time of placing the order.'

exam_qa.q: For a Limit Order on a long position, where is it placed relative to current price?
exam_qa.a: BELOW the current price — the price must first drop to or below the specified level for the buy order to execute.
exam_qa.why: 'When seeking to open a long position, the trader enters a Limit order below the current price; thus, the price must first drop to or below the specified level.'

exam_qa.q: For a Stop Order on a long position, where is it placed relative to current price?
exam_qa.a: ABOVE the current price — anticipating continuation of the upward move (breakout buy).
exam_qa.why: 'For a long position, the trader places a Stop order above the current price.'

exam_qa.q: What is the main risk of a Stop Order?
exam_qa.a: Slippage — it acts as a market order when triggered, so in low liquidity or volatile markets the fill price may be worse than expected.
exam_qa.why: 'Essentially, a stop order acts as a market order, executed at the best available price. Therefore, traders must consider potential slippage, especially in low liquidity or volatile markets.'

exam_qa.q: What problem does the Stop Limit Order solve compared to a plain Stop Order?
exam_qa.a: It prevents negative slippage by triggering a Limit price (the 'worst' price willing to accept) when the Stop level is reached.
exam_qa.why: 'A Stop Limit order solves this problem by triggering the Limit price when the Stop level is reached, which is the worst price a trader is willing to accept.'

exam_qa.q: What is a Stop Loss?
exam_qa.a: A type of Stop order that closes a position when price moves against the trader, representing the maximum loss the trader is willing to accept.
exam_qa.why: 'A Stop Loss order is a type of Stop order used to close a position when the price does not move as anticipated... It represents the maximum loss the trader is willing to accept on a trade.'

exam_qa.q: For a long position, where is the Stop Loss typically placed?
exam_qa.a: Below the entry price.
exam_qa.why: 'For a long position, the Stop Loss is typically placed below the entry price.'

exam_qa.q: What is a Take Profit?
exam_qa.a: A Limit order designed to close an open position at the trader's target profit level. For a long: placed above entry price.
exam_qa.why: 'A Take Profit order is a limit order designed to close an open position that is moving in the direction of the trader's trade entry.'

exam_qa.q: What is a Trailing Stop?
exam_qa.a: A dynamic Stop Loss that adjusts automatically in the trader's favour as price moves. Activates only after a predetermined profit level is reached.
exam_qa.why: 'A specific type of Stop Loss order is the Trailing Stop... it is a dynamic order that adjusts according to the asset's price movement in the trader's favour. It activates only when a predetermined profit level is reached.'

exam_qa.q: If a Trailing Stop is set at 25 points and the trade reaches 25 points of profit, what happens to the Stop Loss?
exam_qa.a: The Stop Loss moves to the Break Even level and continues moving with each further favourable price movement.
exam_qa.why: 'If a trader sets a Trailing Stop at 25 points, once this profit threshold is achieved, the Stop Loss will adjust to the Break Even level. Subsequently, it continues to move with each further favourable movement.'

exam_qa.q: What four prices does a candlestick display?
exam_qa.a: Open, Close, Low, and High prices within the specified timeframe.
exam_qa.why: 'On a candlestick chart, traders can observe... the open, close, low, and high prices.'

exam_qa.q: What does volatility reflect besides price fluctuation range?
exam_qa.a: The degree of uncertainty and risk associated with an investment.
exam_qa.why: 'It also reflects the degree of uncertainty and risk associated with an investment.'

exam_qa.q: What is a Broker?
exam_qa.a: A company that arranges trades in the financial markets for traders in return for a commission.
exam_qa.why: Exact FTMO Academy definition: 'A company that arranges trades in the financial markets for traders in return for a commission.'

exam_qa.q: What is Modern Prop Trading?
exam_qa.a: Firms that seek out novice traders and provide tools, education, and environment to develop trading skills — without financial risk to the clients.
exam_qa.why: 'Modern prop trading firms are companies whose goal is to seek out novice traders and provide them with the tools, education, and environment to experiment with their trading skills... without risk of financial loss on the part of their clients.'

exam_qa.q: When you open a long position on EUR/USD, what are you speculating on?
exam_qa.a: An increase in the value of the euro and/or a decrease in the value of the US dollar.
exam_qa.why: 'When you open a long position on the EUR/USD currency pair, you are speculating on an increase in the value of the euro and/or a decrease in the value of the US dollar.'

exam_qa.q: When you open a short position on EUR/USD, what are you speculating on?
exam_qa.a: A decrease in the value of the euro and/or an increase in the value of the US dollar.
exam_qa.why: 'Conversely, when you open a short position on this currency pair, you are speculating on a decrease in the value of the euro and/or an increase in the value of the US dollar.'

trading_agent_insights: Bid/Ask spread is the broker's implicit cost — agent must factor spread into pip-based calculations when evaluating RRR.
Stop Order triggers at market price (slippage risk) — agent uses Limit Orders for controlled entries, Stop Orders only for breakout confirmation.
Trailing Stop is the preferred mechanism for locking profits — agent already implements this ('Trail SL to breakeven after 1:1 reached').
Standard lot = 100,000 units — agent lot size calculation for 0.5% risk must always reference this base unit.
Margin is the broker's collateral requirement — agent must never allow open trades to reduce free margin below safe threshold.
Pip value is fixed at 0.0001 for most pairs — agent uses this for precise SL/TP distance calculation in pip terms.

key_concepts.trade_execution: The act of placing a Buy or Sell order in the market. Trading is basically very simple — no specific skills are required to execute the trade itself.
key_concepts.pre_trade_checklist: Before each trade you must know: (1) WHY you are entering it, (2) at WHAT PRICE you will enter, (3) what POSITION SIZE you will choose.
key_concepts.position_size: Determines the size of your profit or loss. Choosing the right position size is critical before every trade.
key_concepts.stop_loss_purpose: Set to PREVENT EXCESSIVE LOSSES — it is crucial to set before every trade.
key_concepts.take_profit_purpose: Set to SECURE YOUR DESIRED PROFIT — it is crucial to set before every trade.
key_concepts.long_term_profitability_rule: For long-term profitability, your Take Profit should EXCEED your Stop Loss — ensuring profits outweigh losses.
key_concepts.buy_order: Used when you want to speculate on a RISING price.
key_concepts.sell_order: Used when you want to speculate on a FALLING price.

key_rules: Trading is basically very simple — you do NOT need specific skills to execute the trade itself.
Before every trade: know WHY, at WHAT PRICE, and with WHAT POSITION SIZE.
Position size determines the size of profit or loss.
Stop Loss is CRUCIAL — prevents excessive losses.
Take Profit is CRUCIAL — secures desired profit.
For long-term profitability: Take Profit MUST EXCEED Stop Loss.
Speculating on rising price → click BUY.
Speculating on falling price → click SELL.

exam_qa.q: What should you know before executing a trade?
exam_qa.a: Why you are entering the trade, at what price, and what position size you will choose.
exam_qa.why: 'Before each trade you should make sure you know why you are entering it, at what price and what position size you will choose.'

exam_qa.q: What determines the size of your profit or loss?
exam_qa.a: The size of the position (position size).
exam_qa.why: 'The size of the position will determine the size of your profit or loss.'

exam_qa.q: What is the purpose of setting a Stop Loss?
exam_qa.a: To prevent excessive losses.
exam_qa.why: 'It's crucial to set a Stop Loss to prevent excessive losses.'

exam_qa.q: What is the purpose of setting a Take Profit?
exam_qa.a: To secure your desired profit.
exam_qa.why: '[Set] a Take Profit to secure your desired profit.'

exam_qa.q: For long-term profitability, what is the relationship between Take Profit and Stop Loss?
